title: Case Study: Building a Robust File Reader
verdict: PASS — all six checks clear: clean H1/no-frontmatter with valid sidecar and the six fixed sections in order; length within the DEEP budget (~1970 words reported, ~10 min); citations [1][2][3] all present with primary [1] cited and a matching bibliography, no orphans or fabricated markers; no forward references (7.1/7.2/3.1/4.1/5.2 synthesis is expected, no week 8+ material); the load-bearing worked program is intact and runnable Python 3 with output that exactly matches the traced result (2 valid, 3 bad rows); beginner tone with the conveyor-belt inspector analogy present.

checks.detail: First non-empty line is the H1 '# Case Study: Building a Robust File Reader' (line 1); no leading YAML frontmatter. Sidecar reading.meta.json PRESENT and valid — carries word_count (1970), estimated_read_minutes (10), citations_used ([1,2,3]), sections (all six). Exactly the six required '##' headings appear in order: Overview, Key Concepts, Worked Example, In Practice, Key Takeaways, References (none added/renamed/dropped). The inline ```mermaid pipeline diagram inside Key Concepts (lines 15-45) is expected and not counted as a violation.

Reported word_count 1970 sits inside the DEEP band 1800-2200. Reading-aloud minutes ~1970/230 ≈ 8.6 min; sidecar estimated_read_minutes 10 (at 200 wpm) is consistent. Prose density across all six sections corroborates a DEEP-length reading; no over/under-length flag.

Markers [1], [2], [3] all appear inline (e.g. Overview line 5, Move 4 line 163, In Practice lines 212-216). Primary resource [1] (Real Python — python-csv) is cited multiple times. Bibliography (lines 229-231) has exactly three entries matching the three inline markers — no orphan markers, no orphan entries, no fabricated markers. Minor non-blocking note: reference [3] in the reading resolves to 'realpython.com/python-csv/#parsing-csv-files...' whereas topic.resources.json lists the 'applied' role as 'realpython.com/python-interview-problem-parsing-csv-files/'; both are real Real Python URLs, so no fabrication — just a sidecar-vs-bibliography URL drift worth aligning.

All back-references are to prior material: 7.1 (open/with, csv.DictReader/DictWriter, CSV values are strings), 7.2 (try/except, ValueError/KeyError, raise), 3.1 (functions), plus corpus prerequisites 4.1/5.2. This synthesis reuse is expected and correct for a capstone. No week 8+ material referenced.

Sampled 10 assertions (robust=keep-going/skip-and-log; conveyor analogy; per-row try placement is the fragile/robust difference; three validation axes presence/type/range; DictReader restval=None fills short rows; two-collection output; enumerate(start=2) for line numbers; grouped except; DictWriter for clean output; validation extracted to its own function) — every one traces verbatim or paraphrased to topic_corpus.md. 0 unsupported assertions (threshold is >2). Worked program verified runnable Python 3: traced Move 3 validate_row + Move 4 caller over people.csv — Sam(valid), Ada(ValueError 'invalid literal for int() ... thirty'), Lee(raised 'age -5 out of range'), Kai(short row -> email None -> 'email is missing'), Rob(valid). Result: 2 valid, 3 bad, matching the shown 'Read 2 valid rows, 3 bad rows.' summary, the Valid records block, the three error lines (3/4/5) with exact reasons and row dicts, and the people_clean.csv containing only Sam and Rob. Move 3's except (ValueError, KeyError) is correct because validate_row uses row.get() and guards None before .lower(), so no AttributeError escapes. Output is fully consistent with the code.

Beginner register: new terms (robust, fragile, skip-and-log pattern, validation) defined on first use. Required conveyor-belt inspector analogy is present (line 11) and maps cleanly — defective item = bad row, rejects bin with a note = errors[] with reason, belt keeps moving = loop continues. Average sentence length under 25 words with a few longer-but-readable sentences; no academic hedging phrases ('many experts believe', etc.) — 0 occurrences, under the <3 threshold.
